subject: ap-precalculus
title: Exponential Growth Modeling: Bacterial Population, Competing Models & Semi-Log Analysis
stimulus:
A biologist is studying a colony of bacteria growing in a nutrient-rich solution. The biologist measures the population at selected times and records the following data:

| Time $t$ (hours) | Population $P$ (thousands) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 3.1 |
| 2 | 7.4 |
| 5 | 28.6 |
| 8 | 110.3 |
| 11 | 421.7 |

The biologist considers two competing models for the population: an exponential model $E(t) = a \cdot b^t$ and a power model $W(t) = c \cdot t^d$.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 11$ to write two equations that can be used to find the values of constants $a$ and $b$ in the exponential model $E(t) = a \cdot b^t$.

(ii) Find the values of $a$ and $b$ as decimal approximations. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: The first equation gives $a = 3.1$ directly since $b^0 = 1$. For $b$: $\ln b = \frac{\ln(421.7/3.1)}{11} = \frac{\ln(136.03)}{11} \approx \frac{4.913}{11} \approx 0.4466$, so $b \approx e^{0.4466} \approx 1.563$. Accept values in $[1.558, 1.568]$. Note: a student using a different pair of data points will get a slightly different $b$; accept any two-point derivation with correct algebra and a value reasonably close to 1.563.

scoring_points.description: Two correct equations — $a \cdot b^0 = 3.1$, which gives $a = 3.1$; and $3.1 \cdot b^{11} = 421.7$; equivalently $b^{11} = \frac{421.7}{3.1} = 136.03\ldots$

required_elements: Two correct equations — $a \cdot b^0 = 3.1$, which gives $a = 3.1$; and $3.1 \cdot b^{11} = 421.7$; equivalently $b^{11} = \frac{421.7}{3.1} = 136.03\ldots$
alternatives.correct_example: Two correct equations — $a \cdot b^0 = 3.1$, which gives $a = 3.1$; and $3.1 \cdot b^{11} = 421.7$; equivalently $b^{11} = \frac{421.7}{3.1} = 136.03\ldots$

scoring_points.description: Correct values $a = 3.1$ and $b = 136.03^{1/11} \approx 1.563$ (from $b = \left(\frac{421.7}{3.1}\right)^{1/11} = e^{\ln(136.03)/11} \approx e^{0.4466} \approx 1.563$; accept values in $[1.558, 1.568]$); supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step

required_elements: Correct values $a = 3.1$ and $b = 136.03^{1/11} \approx 1.563$ (from $b = \left(\frac{421.7}{3.1}\right)^{1/11} = e^{\ln(136.03)/11} \approx e^{0.4466} \approx 1.563$; accept values in $[1.558, 1.568]$); supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step
alternatives.correct_example: Correct values $a = 3.1$ and $b = 136.03^{1/11} \approx 1.563$ (from $b = \left(\frac{421.7}{3.1}\right)^{1/11} = e^{\ln(136.03)/11} \approx e^{0.4466} \approx 1.563$; accept values in $[1.558, 1.568]$); supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step

parts.prompt:
(i) To determine whether the exponential model $E$ is more appropriate than a linear model for this data, a biologist creates a semi-log plot by graphing $\ln(P)$ versus $t$. Using the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 11$, calculate the slope of the line on the semi-log plot.

(ii) Use the exponential model $E(t) = 3.1 \cdot b^t$ with $b$ from part (a) to estimate the population at $t = 6$ hours. Express your answer as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $\ln(421.7) \approx 6.043$ and $\ln(3.1) \approx 1.131$, giving slope $\approx 4.912/11 \approx 0.4465$. This equals $\ln(b)$ from part (a), consistent. For part (ii), $E(6) = 3.1 \cdot b^6$ where $b \approx 1.563$; $(1.563)^6 \approx 13.77$, so $E(6) \approx 42.7$ thousand. Accept any value derived correctly from the student's $b$ value from part (a).

scoring_points.description: Correct slope — $\frac{\ln(421.7) - \ln(3.1)}{11 - 0} = \frac{6.043 - 1.131}{11} = \frac{4.912}{11} \approx 0.4465$ (accept values in $[0.444, 0.449]$); supporting work must show a quotient using $\ln$ of the population values

required_elements: Correct slope — $\frac{\ln(421.7) - \ln(3.1)}{11 - 0} = \frac{6.043 - 1.131}{11} = \frac{4.912}{11} \approx 0.4465$ (accept values in $[0.444, 0.449]$); supporting work must show a quotient using $\ln$ of the population values
alternatives.correct_example: Correct slope — $\frac{\ln(421.7) - \ln(3.1)}{11 - 0} = \frac{6.043 - 1.131}{11} = \frac{4.912}{11} \approx 0.4465$ (accept values in $[0.444, 0.449]$); supporting work must show a quotient using $\ln$ of the population values

scoring_points.description: Correct estimate — $E(6) = 3.1 \cdot (1.563)^6 \approx 3.1 \cdot 13.77 \approx 42.7$ thousand (accept values in $[40, 46]$ to account for rounding differences in $b$); supporting work must show substitution of $t = 6$ into the model

required_elements: Correct estimate — $E(6) = 3.1 \cdot (1.563)^6 \approx 3.1 \cdot 13.77 \approx 42.7$ thousand (accept values in $[40, 46]$ to account for rounding differences in $b$); supporting work must show substitution of $t = 6$ into the model
alternatives.correct_example: Correct estimate — $E(6) = 3.1 \cdot (1.563)^6 \approx 3.1 \cdot 13.77 \approx 42.7$ thousand (accept values in $[40, 46]$ to account for rounding differences in $b$); supporting work must show substitution of $t = 6$ into the model

parts.prompt:
(i) The biologist's colleague proposes that the population exceeds 500 thousand bacteria sometime between $t = 11$ and $t = 12$ hours. Using the exponential model $E(t) = 3.1 \cdot b^t$ with $b$ from part (a), write and solve an inequality to find the earliest time $t$ at which the population first exceeds 500 thousand. Express your answer as a decimal approximation.

(ii) The colleague also suggests a linear model $L(t) = mt + 3.1$ fitted through $t = 0$ and $t = 11$. Find the value of $m$ as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), the exact threshold time depends on the value of $b$ from part (a). Using $b \approx 1.563$: $\ln(500/3.1)/\ln(1.563) \approx 5.084/0.4466 \approx 11.38$ hours. Award the point for correct inequality setup and logarithm step even if $b$ differs slightly due to rounding. For part (ii), the slope $m = (421.7 - 3.1)/11 \approx 38.05$. Supporting work must show the two data values used and the difference-quotient computation.

scoring_points.description: Correct inequality and solution — $3.1 \cdot b^t > 500 \Rightarrow b^t > \frac{500}{3.1} \Rightarrow t > \frac{\ln(500/3.1)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{\ln(161.29)}{\ln(1.563)} \approx \frac{5.084}{0.4466} \approx 11.38$ hours (accept values in $[11.3, 11.5]$); supporting work must show the inequality setup and logarithm step

required_elements: Correct inequality and solution — $3.1 \cdot b^t > 500 \Rightarrow b^t > \frac{500}{3.1} \Rightarrow t > \frac{\ln(500/3.1)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{\ln(161.29)}{\ln(1.563)} \approx \frac{5.084}{0.4466} \approx 11.38$ hours (accept values in $[11.3, 11.5]$); supporting work must show the inequality setup and logarithm step
alternatives.correct_example: Correct inequality and solution — $3.1 \cdot b^t > 500 \Rightarrow b^t > \frac{500}{3.1} \Rightarrow t > \frac{\ln(500/3.1)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{\ln(161.29)}{\ln(1.563)} \approx \frac{5.084}{0.4466} \approx 11.38$ hours (accept values in $[11.3, 11.5]$); supporting work must show the inequality setup and logarithm step

scoring_points.description: Correct slope $m = \frac{421.7 - 3.1}{11 - 0} = \frac{418.6}{11} \approx 38.05$ (accept values in $[38.0, 38.1]$); supporting work must show the difference quotient using the data values at $t = 0$ and $t = 11$

required_elements: Correct slope $m = \frac{421.7 - 3.1}{11 - 0} = \frac{418.6}{11} \approx 38.05$ (accept values in $[38.0, 38.1]$); supporting work must show the difference quotient using the data values at $t = 0$ and $t = 11$
alternatives.correct_example: Correct slope $m = \frac{421.7 - 3.1}{11 - 0} = \frac{418.6}{11} \approx 38.05$ (accept values in $[38.0, 38.1]$); supporting work must show the difference quotient using the data values at $t = 0$ and $t = 11$
